AS ALWAYS: OUR DEAL
The deal remains exactly the same as last time. I’ll share everything I’ve learned about Suno, AI music, and the dirty tricks behind it. In return, you check out my latest banger, and if it hits, you save it on Spotify. That’s the trade. No newsletter, no Patreon, no middleman.
The track for this episode is called “Isolierter Wolf — Einsam trotz Vernetzung” (Isolated Wolf — Lonely Despite Connection).
https://youtu.be/cRkoK5oy4XQ
A quick look under the hood: The vocals are mostly me rapping. The voice is polished with Melodyne, not AI-generated. The pad sounds were built using Suno. The playthrough, meaning the core instrumental structure the track runs on, was generated in Suno and then chopped, arranged, and heavily processed in Ableton Live. It’s a true hybrid production: a lot of human vocals, a lot of human arrangement, with the AI sitting purely in the sound bed. I’ll show you exactly how I pull this off, step by step, in one of the next parts.
The wolf artwork featured throughout the video was created by the artist Florian Klinger. No prompt, no Midjourney, no Suno. Real handwork. Go drop a save, then let’s keep going.
SPARK: A PROGRAM THAT RAISES HARD QUESTIONS
What Suno promises its Spark artists, what the fine print actually says, and why the answer to “should I apply?” is far less black-and-white than either side wants you to believe.
On June 25, 2026, Suno launched a program called Spark. An incubator for independent artists promising grants ranging from thousands to tens of thousands of dollars, marketing support, mentoring, and early access to new features. Over at suno.com/spark, it sounds exactly like what indie artists have been waiting for: platform backing, cash flow, visibility.
But a lot happened just three days prior. The music industry erupted when the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA), backed by major labels, launched a massive copyright infringement lawsuit against Suno, explicitly listing hundreds of tracks, including those from prominent artists like Solána Imani Rowe (SZA), as proof of unauthorized training data usage. Producer Kenny Beats immediately dropped a viral post reacting to the fallout, triggering a massive public relations (PR) storm for the platform. Simultaneously, the US powerhouse law firm Hagens Berman, infamous for locking down a $260 billion settlement against Big Tobacco, officially joined an ongoing class-action lawsuit filed by independent creators. And just three weeks before that, Suno closed a $400 million Series D funding round at a $5.4 billion valuation.
The timing is loud. Whether it’s pure coincidence is another debate. Maybe Spark had been in development for months and the launch date just happened to land on that exact week. Maybe not. I can’t prove it; I can only document it.
What can be documented, however, is what the program claims, what the fine print states, and the glaring gap between marketing promises and contractual reality. That is the core of this article. I’m not leaving you with a recommendation at the end. I’m leaving you with a question.
WHAT SPARK PROMISES
From the official blog post by Paul Sinclair (Chief Music Officer, formerly Atlantic Records) and Rosie Nguyen (Head of Creative Economy and Monetization) on June 25, 2026:
“Spark is designed to bridge this gap. We want to encourage artists to create, while also giving them a way to build an audience and community on Suno. Think of Spark as the ignition point for your ideas. Our role is to support your project, not own it.” (suno.com/blog/introducing-spark)
Specifically, selected participants receive:

• Grants ranging from “thousands to tens of thousands of dollars” (Billboard via KJWL)

• Marketing support via influencers, paid advertising, visuals, events, and PR

• Writing camps with established artists

• A dedicated Suno Partner Manager

• Editorial placements and Artist Spotlights across Suno channels

• Early access to upcoming features, free Suno Premier access, and song credits
And Suno doubles down on their FAQ page:
“You retain creative control of your songs and videos as long as they are high-quality, original projects that follow our ToS.” (suno.com/spark)
“Artists will retain creative control and commercial rights of their works.” (suno.com/blog/introducing-spark)
That’s the storefront. Now let’s peek into the backroom.
WHAT THE FINE PRINT SAYS
This is where it gets interesting, and tricky at the same time. The complete, unredacted Spark Terms and Conditions are not accessible anywhere as a standalone public document. The fine print is nowhere to be found on the Spark landing page. What we do know comes from investigative reporting by Music Business Worldwide (MBW), Stereogum, The Verge, and a rigorous legal breakdown with 47 upvotes in Suno’s own subreddit. These sources quote the terms partly verbatim, partly paraphrased. Where sources conflict, I will point it out.
Four clauses appear consistently across all reports, and they warrant a very careful reading.
1. The “Good Vibes Only” Clause
Hidden within the Spark terms is a section explicitly titled “Good Vibes Only.” Stereogum quotes it as follows:
“During the Term and thereafter, You will not at any time make any statements or representations, either directly or indirectly, whether orally or in writing, that disparage Spark or its participants. This is about maintaining mutual respect, and does not prevent you from speaking honestly about your experience in the program.” (Stereogum)
Music Business Worldwide quotes a slightly broader, more aggressive variation of the same clause:
“[…] will not at any time make any statements or representations, either directly or indirectly, whether orally or in writing, that portrays Suno, Suno personnel, and/or any Suno products or services in a negative light.” (MBW — Music Business Worldwide)
Two details here are critical.
First: “during the Term and thereafter”: this clause has no expiration date.
Second: it simultaneously claims that honest feedback regarding your personal experience is permitted. How exactly “speaking honestly about your experience” and “doing nothing that portrays Suno in a negative light” are supposed to coexist is anyone’s guess. In practice, the burden of interpretation lies entirely on the creator.
2. The NIL Clause
NIL stands for “Name, Image, and Likeness.” Here too, public analysis relies heavily on MBW:
“[Suno may use] your name and likeness for marketing and promotional purposes during the Term and thereafter. [The license extends] to Suno owned or operated channels, press and other digital media, including in derivative works.” (MBW)”
This language grants Suno the unrestricted right to exploit the participant’s name and face for marketing long after the program ends, explicitly expanding to “derivative works” across their own platforms, PR, and digital media.
What “derivative works” actually means when dealing with a generative AI corporation is highly up for debate. It could mean a standard marketing mock-up.
It could also mean training an AI-generated digital avatar model of your persona.
The contract leaves this open-ended.
3. The Remix Mandate and 60-Day Exclusivity
From the official Spark requirements:
“Music must be made with Suno as part of the creative process and must live on Suno.” (suno.com/spark)
Submitted tracks must remain on Suno and be unlocked for public remixing.
This goes way beyond standard promotional requirements: other Suno users are legally allowed to alter, regenerate, and spin off your original work.
Additionally, a 60-day exclusivity window applies after your final submission: during this period, participants are barred from entering any paid or formal agreements with competing AI music entities like Udio, Riffusion, or similar platforms. This specific clause is documented via the community analysis on Reddit (r/SunoAI), rather than official primary press releases.
4. The Standard ToS Operates in the Background
Spark participants are still bound by the regular Suno Terms of Service. These terms contain an incredibly broad license granting Suno the right to all submitted content:
“[…] to use, reproduce, store, modify, distribute, create derivative works based on, perform, display, communicate, transmit and otherwise make available any and all Content […] and any rights you may have in your Voice Model […] in connection with the provision, use, monetization, promotion, marketing, and improvement of our products and services, including the Service and the artificial intelligence and machine learning models related to the Service.” (suno.com/terms-of-service)
According to the text, this license is “perpetual, irrevocable, worldwide, non-exclusive, sublicensable, royalty-free.”
It explicitly includes using your data for AI model training.
There is no opt-out.
Furthermore, the standard ToS contains a strict class-action waiver paired with a binding arbitration clause. Any dispute between you and Suno must be fought individually, stripping away your right to join a class-action lawsuit. While a 30-day opt-out window for this arbitration clause exists (fineprint.to), exercising it requires rapid, proactive written notice within 30 days of creating your account or accepting a ToS update.
THE ASSESSMENT, WITHOUT THE HAMMER
Let’s look at this soberly, without dramatic conclusions.
What these clauses are legally: They are industry-standard boilerplate mechanisms under US contract law. You find non-disparagement clauses in influencer contracts, NDA frameworks, boyband agreements, and corporate sports sponsorships. NIL clauses are the bedrock of any modern marketing deal. Class-action waivers have been heavily enforced and recognized by US courts for over a decade.
What changes them inside an AI ecosystem: The reach. In a traditional recording contract, a “derivative work” usually means a radio edit, a club remix, or a music video. Inside a generative AI ecosystem, a “derivative work” can theoretically mean any future AI model output that retains and reflects elements of your original work. The clause isn’t inherently “evil”. It is simply technologically under-specified. A boilerplate NIL clause written by a corporate attorney in 2018 carries an entirely different practical gravity in 2026 because the technology underneath it has changed.
What the German (I am a German lawyer, no expert in US Law) legal lens says: For a creator based in Europe or Germany, the enforceability of several of these clauses is highly questionable under strict consumer protection laws and standard terms regulations (such as §§ 305 et seq. of the German Civil Code = BGB).
A perpetual, lifetime non-disparagement clause would likely fail the fairness test under § 307 BGB.
An irrevocable, everlasting NIL license directly clashes with § 22 of the KUG (the German Art Copyright Act) regarding the right to one’s own image and the deeply entrenched right to revoke personal consent.
Class-action waivers for everyday consumers face massive formal hurdles under § 1031 Abs. 5 of the German Code of Civil Procedure (ZPO), hurdles that a simple web interface checkbox cannot clear.
But here is the catch: Pointing out that a clause is “likely unenforceable under domestic German law” is only a valid shield if you have the financial runway, time, and appetite to litigate a cross-border contractual dispute against a well-capitalized Silicon Valley entity.
What these clauses achieve for the company: They insulate Suno from PR liabilities caused by artists they are actively funding. They secure the necessary intellectual property infrastructure to market the program effectively. They ensure that their funded cohort doesn’t simultaneously prop up direct market competitors. From the operational perspective of an enterprise currently fighting multiple massive industry lawsuits, every single one of these clauses makes perfect corporate s
